data.title: B2Gold: A Weaker 2026 Setting Up A Stronger Future
data.description:
Introduction

B2Gold (BTG) is a Canadian gold producer with operations across four mines located in Mali, Philippines, Namibia, and Canada. B2Gold has faced a turbulent period with security concerns in Mali and rising development costs at its Goose Mine in Canada putting the shares under pressure.

These issues have now come to pass. Operations in Mali continue uninterrupted, with agreements reached under the country's new mining code, and the Goose mine is in production and currently ramping up. Supported by the higher gold price, the shares rose 89% in 2025.

Despite this strong return, the shares still lagged the wider VanEck Gold Miners Index (GDX) which rose 155% in 2025. This underperformance reflects continued investor concerns around Mali and weak execution at the company.

2026 is set to remain a transition year. Cost guidance on the surface appeared weak, but dig in closer and it's all about setting up for a stronger 2027 and reflective of higher royalties from the current gold price. With the gold prepay agreement expiring in June 2026 this offers another boost. This allows 66,000 ounces of quarterly production to be sold at market prices, currently ~$5,100/oz, compared to the $2,191/oz in the prepay, suggesting a significant uplift to revenue could be coming.

With this in mind, let's explore B2Gold's latest earnings, cover the guidance for 2026, and ultimately explain why I believe B2Gold shares still represent good value.

Q4 Earnings

B2Gold recently reported their Q4 '25 earnings, reporting quarterly production of 303,029 oz of gold. This marked an increase of ~117,000 oz (+63%) from the previous year. This increase was driven by the Goose Mine in Canada, which entered production earlier in 2025 and produced 38,616 oz in Q4.

Fekola, located in Mali, had a strong end to the year; production rose 95% from a year earlier to reach 163,720 oz. This increase largely came from improved grades (2.29 g/t vs. 1.17 g/t), and a small increase in recovery rates (92.4% vs. 91.9%). This came as a higher-grade zone of the mine was reached, and gold production from the 2025 permitted Fekola underground began.

Masbate in the Philippines, continued its strong production record, producing 49,900 oz in Q4. Whilst this only represents a ~1% increase from 2024 levels, this is better than initially expected due to higher mill throughput and higher recoveries (78% vs. 74.1%), offset by a slight fall in grades (0.91 g/t vs. 0.95 g/t).

B2Gold's final mine, Otjikoto in Namibia, also had a strong performance. Despite open-pit mining having ended and the mine processing open-pit stockpiles, production fell only slightly compared to 2024 coming in at 50,793 oz (-3%). Falling grades (1.91 g/t vs. 2.10 g/t) were offset by a 6% rise in tonnes milled, with recoveries remaining stable (98.7%). Ore processing from stockpiles is set to continue at the mine until the Antelope deposit is fully developed, expected to begin production in late 2027.

For the full year, production came in at 979,604 oz, within B2Gold's guidance range of 970,000 and 1,075,000 ounces.

B2Gold achieved an average realized gold price of $3,718 per share in the quarter, 40% higher than Q4 ‘24, as gold continued its upward rally. For many, this gold price may seem low, especially considering all the headlines about gold at over $5,000 an ounce. The reason for this is a gold-prepay agreement that requires delivery of 66,000 ounces a quarter at a price of $2,191. This is set to run until June 2026 and represents the delivery of 264,775 ounces, with the revenue being realized upon the gold's delivery.

283,490 ounces of gold were sold during the quarter, 20 Koz lower than production, attributed to shipment timings from Fekola. Accounting for this, revenue came in at $1.05 billion, 111% higher than in Q4 '24.

Looking at cash flow, B2Gold reported cash flow from operations of $290.6 million, a significant increase from the $120.5 million reported a year earlier, as the company benefited from the higher gold price and increased production.

On the balance sheet side, B2Gold ended the quarter with $380.4 million in cash and a total debt of $598 million. With cash requirements for capex greatly reduced following Goose in production and the current cash flow from gold at over $5,000/oz, I believe the current balance sheet looks strong, with no major solvency risks. Once the gold prepay agreement ends in June, B2Gold should experience a substantial cash flow acceleration, strengthening the balance sheet.

Also on the balance sheet is a portfolio of investments valued at ~$600 million. Like B2Gold, these investments have been performing strongly due to the rising gold price, alongside company-specific factors. This also provides B2Gold with some optionality by providing potential future growth opportunities.

On the costs front, B2Gold reported all-in sustaining costs [AISC] coming in at $1,754 per ounce, a $106/oz increase from a year earlier. The biggest contributor to this rise was the Goose Mine, which recorded an AISC of $2,249/oz, the highest of B2Gold's mines. With commercial production only declared in Q4 last year, costs should fall as production ramps up and capital expenditures to increase crushing capacity are complete. Goose will have a higher cost in 2026, before a sharp fall in AISC is expected in 2027.

At Fekola, AISC was $1,903/oz (-15%). This was higher than expected but is down to the higher gold price resulting in increased royalty costs, and the sales timing of 20,500 oz of gold being pushed into Q1.

Masbate AISC was $1,230/oz (+12%) during the quarter as increasing royalties increased costs. An increase in AISC was also observed at Otjikoto, with a rise to $1,085/oz (+19%) as increased capital expenditures on the Wolfshag underground mine development and increased royalties pushed up costs.

Overall, this translated to a net profit of $180.3 million, an improvement from the net loss of $9 million a year earlier. On an adjusted basis, earnings also increased, coming in at $147.3 million, up from $17.4 million a year earlier, and equivalent to $0.11 per share, a $0.07 miss from expectations.

For B2Gold, this quarter marks the continued growth of the company, not just from higher gold prices, but from increased production. With the Goose mine ramping up and hitting 2025 production guidance, B2Gold is setting itself up for a positive future.

Recent Developments

2026 Guidance: Weak on the Surface, Strategic Beneath

Alongside their results, B2Gold released their guidance for 2026. On the surface, guidance appears weak with lower production and a sharp increase in costs. However, the figures reflect a transition year as B2Gold undertakes several major investments and continues ramping up the Goose mine.

For 2026, B2Gold guides for production of 820,000-970,000 ounces of gold, below the 979,604 ounces produced in 2025. Production is set to be driven higher as the Goose mine continues its ramp-up, with the midpoint of production guidance at 200,000 oz.

All other mines are expected to see falls in production; Otjikoto is guided at 80,000 oz (2025: 199,139), a sharp fall from 2025 following the end of open-pit mining activities; Masbate is guided at 180,000 oz (2025: 196,526 oz), a small fall from 2025 largely due to a lower expected recovery rate; and Fekola is guided at 435,000 oz (2025: 530,739) due to the timing of mine stripping.

Costs are where the headline numbers appear concerning. B2Gold guides to cash operating costs of $1,155–$1,280/oz and AISC of $2,400–$2,580/oz, significantly higher than the $1,584/oz achieved in 2025.

This, though, is largely due to transitional factors. Deferred stripping at the Fekola complex in Mali is raising near-term costs but should unlock higher-grade ore in future years. Second, Goose is only partially ramped up, meaning fixed costs are spread over fewer ounces, and significant capital expenditure is still required to upgrade the mill. At Otjikoto, open-pit mining has ended, and it's now primarily processing lower-grade stockpiles. Otjikoto's production should rise and AISC fall once development of the Antelope deposit is complete, with this deposit set to see average production of 110,000 oz annually between 2029 and 2032 at an AISC of only $1,095/oz.

Finally, the gold price itself is pushing up costs. B2Gold uses a gold price assumption of $5,000/oz, resulting in significant royalty costs: $525/oz overall, rising to $910/oz at Fekola. For every $100 fall in the gold price, AISC is expected to fall $12.

Whilst AISC may be high this year, at current gold prices this still results in high operating margins and strong profitability. More importantly, this is likely the peak of AISC for the next few years. In 2027, higher grade ore will see Fekola production rise and reduced stripping required, a new crusher at Goose will reduce required capex spending and see costs spread over higher production, and high-grade ore from the Antelope deposit at Otjikoto will lead to a lower AISC.

The guidance is quite clear: B2Gold is facing a higher cost year of elevated costs and uneven production to set it up for a stronger future. Instead of being in different years, these higher costs are concentrated in 2026. If all proceeds as planned, B2Gold should exit 2026 with increased production, positioning it for a much stronger 2027 where AISC should fall.

A New CEO

CEO Clive Johnson is retiring and set to be replaced by current CFO Michael Cinnamond.

While the stock has seen a strong performance under his leadership, rising 89% in 2025, it has undoubtedly underperformed peers, with the wider VanEck Gold Miners Index up 155% in the same period.

The CEO cannot be entirely blamed for this underperformance; cost overruns at Goose were driven by a high inflation period necessitating a gold prepay just before gold prices soared. Significant uncertainty in Mali, with a new mining code and the arrest of other miners executives, certainly put the shares under additional pressure despite B2Gold successfully reaching an agreement.

It seems by announcing the existing CFO as a replacement, the board wanted continuity from someone who knows the business rather than some outsider. You'd hope that a former CFO would also bring increased capital discipline to the business and improved capital allocation. At the current share price trading well below peer multiples, you'd hope there would be a focus on buybacks in the future.

My main concern with appointing a CFO is whether there is less of a focus on operational performance and more on the financial side. One hopes that the new CEO is willing to listen to the wider management team, who are more experienced on the operational side of the business, while balancing this with good capital allocation.

There remains some uncertainty at this stage as to the scale of the changes, if any, a new CEO will bring. But one can only hope they allow B2Gold's performance to improve to no longer lag peers.

Valuation

With the gold price reaching a record high this year, B2Gold's share price, like many of its peers, had a strong rally, before its recent weakness. With all gold miners' earnings reliant on the price of gold, a peer valuation is appropriate to get a relative valuation. With all miners having large differences in cash and debt levels, I take account of this by opting for an enterprise value to EBITDA multiple comparison.

I opted to compare to peers Gold Fields (GFI), AngloGold Ashanti (AU), Equinox Gold (EQX), Barrick Mining (B), Agnico Eagle (AEM), and IAMGOLD (IAG). These were chosen as they are all in-production miners; some have overlap with B2Gold in terms of jurisdictions of operation, and they provide varying sizes of miners to compare to.

B2Gold currently trades at an EV to EBITDA multiple of 3.076, the lowest multiple compared to its peers. This, I believe, is unjustified. B2Gold has successfully opened its Goose mine in Canada, a tier-one jurisdiction, and now operates across four jurisdictions. Although B2Gold warrants a significant discount due to its significant exposure to Mali and will never trade at the levels of a pure tier-one jurisdictional miner like Agnico Eagle, this discount does appear excessive, with most peers trading at a multiple around 5.67% higher than B2Gold's current multiple.

To determine a price value for B2Gold, I will calculate the free cash flow and apply a multiple to this. Given the 264,768 ounces of gold prepayment due between July 2025 and June 2026, I will take what I believe to be the target level production for 2027.

I assume annualized production from Q4 '26 onwards of 1.17 million ounces, made up of 300,000 ounces from Goose, Masbate producing 180,000 ounces, 80,000 ounces from Otjikoto, and 610,000 ounces from Fekola, made up of 180,000 ounces from Fekola Regional and 440,000 ounces from the existing Fekola Mine.

At a gold price of $5,000/oz, I forecast an AISC for Q4 '26 onwards of $1,814/oz. This was calculated by using the 2026 guidance for Otjikoto and Masbate, given production is set to remain stable during the period, and a forecast increase on Goose's life of mine plan given increased royalty costs, and assuming Fekola AISC falls back to 2025 levels as higher production comes online with reduced stripping, again adjusted higher due to increased royalty costs.

I adjust the AISC by $12 for every $100 change in the gold price, the same sensitivity factor used by B2Gold in their 2026 guidance. I assume a 30% tax rate and $0 million in interest costs. As of Q4 2025, B2Gold had $380 million in cash and a total debt of $598 million. Given B2Gold's free cash flow generation, I expect this net debt to become a net-cash position within the next 12 months, justifying a zero-interest cost assumption.

Using a gold price of $3,600 an ounce, 30% below the current gold price to give a margin of safety, I anticipate cash flow per share of $1.20. I apply a cash flow multiplier of x6, just below the average of the mining sector, taking into account that following commercial production at Goose, B2Gold is a more diversified miner but also accounting for the higher risks that come from the Mali operations. This gives a target share price of $7.20 per share, 42% higher than the current share price.

I continue to believe that B2Gold remains a buy at this price, though I am downgrading from a strong buy following the recent rally. When I last gave a Strong Buy rating in November, the share price was $4.10, with a similar forecast target price estimate; they now trade at $5.10, leaving less upside.

The recent rise in the oil price potentially puts more upward pressure on operating costs given mining's energy-intensive nature, and jurisdictional risk from Mali will continue to weigh on valuation.

That said, with Goose now in production, a strong balance sheet, and other expansion projects in the works, I am happy to hold B2Gold's shares and believe they remain a buy.

The biggest risk for B2Gold is the same as any other gold miner: the price of gold and its corresponding impact on the revenue and earnings of B2Gold, which greatly influence the share price.

A further important risk is the jurisdictional risk B2Gold faces. The large exposure to Mali, a high-risk mining jurisdiction with a history of taking control of mines and the arrest of gold mining executives, means B2Gold may face sudden changes in terms of regulations and uncertainty. Despite this, B2Gold does appear to have a good working relationship with Mali's government, reaching an agreement with regard to the new mining codes and the recent granting of the permit for Fekola underground. Whilst this seems positive, with Mali having seized other mines, investors hearing that B2Gold has operations in Mali will always weigh on the valuation.

Conclusion

B2Gold exited 2025 with momentum, with both production and revenues at record highs as the Goose mine reached commercial production.

2026 guidance on the surface appeared weak, but there has to be an understanding that this will be a transition year. It's all about setting up for a stronger 2027 and reflective of higher royalties from the current gold price. With the gold prepay agreement expiring in June 2026 this offers another boost. This allows 66,000 ounces of quarterly production to be sold at market prices, currently ~$5,100/oz, compared to the $2,191/oz in the prepay, suggesting a significant uplift to revenue could be coming.

At current prices, B2Gold trades at a valuation well below its peers, a valuation that looks increasingly unjustified as Goose ramps up. The balance sheet is strong, production is growing, and once the gold prepay completes this year, free cash flow will hit the accelerator.

While Mali will continue to weigh on valuation and the rising oil price may increase costs, the combination of B2Gold setting itself up to grow production makes the recent pullback look like a potential opportunity. For this reason, I consider the shares to have a "buy" rating.

data.title: CIO Notebook: Stagflation Concerns Mount As Oil Prices And Unemployment Rise
data.description:
By Shannon L. Saccocia, CFA

We remain constructive on risk assets, seeing selective opportunities to modestly lengthen duration in fixed income and to begin dollar-cost averaging in equities given the volatility.

February non-farm payrolls were released today, missed estimates meaningfully, and added fuel to a fire already burning hot following the escalation in the Middle East. Payrolls declined by -92k in the month, well below expectations for a +55k gain; this is the weakest payrolls print since October and the sharpest decline in private sector payrolls (-86k) since 2020. In addition, there was a further downward revision to December’s payrolls, from +48k to -17k, with January lower as well by -4k; this implies that trailing three-month payrolls growth stands at less than +6k.

Breaking down the data, the decline in health care hiring (-28k) was the primary driver of this month’s weakness; the reversal of the strongly positive trend exacerbated the weakening in other industries at the aggregate level. Construction (-11k) and manufacturing (-12k) both fell in February, offsetting gains in January, and government payrolls (-6k) continued to move lower despite already meaningful losses over the prior twelve months. There were few bright spots in this month’s report, with the most notable gains in financial activities (+10k) and social assistance (+9k).

In addition, the unemployment rate ticked back up to 4.4%, even with a meaningful decline in the participation rate from 62.5% to 62.0%. More concerning was the decline in employed persons of -185k, while the number of unemployed rose by +203k in the month. The unemployment rate increased across most demographic categories, reversing last month’s trend of broadening employment gains. Despite the downward payroll pressure, average hourly earnings rose by +0.4% month-over-month and +3.8% year-over-year, while average hours worked were steady at 34.3.

Admittedly, there are several factors that likely amplified the weaker trend in today’s report. Specifically, a strike at Kaiser Permanente weighed on health care, and the impact of widespread winter weather conditions is likely attributable to the weakness in construction and transportation & warehousing. (Admittedly, winter weather likely impacted retail sales more meaningfully; January retail sales declined by -0.2%, missing expectations for a flat print.) Even adjusting for these transitory factors, however, today’s report points to continued weakness in private payrolls, although admittedly there is minimal evidence of a read-through to the oft-touted AI disintermediation theme.

Our view is that today’s release has increased the complexity of the situation for the Fed meaningfully. WTI crude is trading close to $90 per barrel in the early hours of today’s session, and with President Donald Trump’s statement that there will be no deal with Iran until “unconditional surrender,” there are valid concerns that an extended period of higher energy prices will transmit to consumers and businesses and slow the pace of disinflation. However, higher prices at some point will also result in demand destruction, which would slow overall growth and release some of the potential economic pressure.

More importantly, continued weakness in the U.S. labor market likely offsets some of the likely shorter-term inflation concerns for the Fed, and, as a result, we still believe the Fed will cut at least two times in 2026. Should meaningful de-escalation in the Middle East come within the next 2-4 weeks, we surmise that the Fed could act as soon as June to combat labor market weakening. As such, we remain constructive on risk assets, with the acknowledgement that there may be opportunities to lengthen duration modestly in the fixed income markets, and for those clients with funds earmarked for equity investment, there exist opportunities to begin dollar-cost averaging in this volatile environment.

Important Information

This material is provided for informational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice, or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold a security. This material is general in nature and is not directed to any category of investors and should not be regarded as individualized, a recommendation, investment advice or a suggestion to engage in or refrain from any investment-related course of action. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm as a whole. Neuberger Berman products and services may not be available in all jurisdictions or to all client types. Diversification does not guarantee profit or protect against loss in declining markets. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Investments in private equity are speculative and involve a higher degree of risk than more traditional investments. Investments in private equity are intended for sophisticated investors only. Unless otherwise indicated, returns shown reflect reinvestment of dividends and distributions. Indexes are unmanaged and are not available for direct investment. Investing entails risks, including possible loss of principal. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Portfolio positioning views expressed herein are those of Neuberger Berman’s Private Wealth Investment Group, which may include those of the Neuberger Berman’s Asset Allocation Committee. Asset allocation and positioning views are based on a hypothetical reference portfolio. The Private Wealth Investment Group analyzes market and economic indicators to develop asset allocation strategies. The Private Wealth Investment Group works in partnership with the Office of the CIO. The Private Wealth Investment Group also consults regularly with portfolio managers and investment officers across the firm. The Asset Allocation Committee is comprised of professionals across multiple disciplines, including equity and fixed income strategists and portfolio managers. The Asset Allocation Committee reviews and sets long-term asset allocation models, establishes preferred near-term tactical asset class allocations and, upon request, reviews asset allocations for large, diversified mandates. Asset Allocation Committee members are polled on asset classes and the positional views are representative of an Asset Allocation Committee consensus. The views of the Asset Allocation Committee and the Private Wealth Investment Group may not reflect the views of the firm as a whole and Neuberger Berman advisers and portfolio managers may take contrary positions to the views of the Asset Allocation Committee or the Private Wealth Investment Group. The Asset Allocation Committee and the Private Wealth Investment Group views do not constitute a prediction or projection of future events or future market behavior. Defensive positioning generally means an underweight bias on allocations to risk assets such as equities and alternatives. Positioning views may change over time without notice and actual client positioning may vary significantly. Discussion of yield characteristics or total returns of different asset classes are for illustrative purposes only. Such asset classes, such as equities and fixed income, may have significantly different overall risk-return characteristics which should be consider before investing.

The information in this material may contain projections, market outlooks or other forward-looking statements regarding future events, including economic, asset class and market outlooks or expectations, and is only current as of the date indicated. There is no assurance that such events, outlook and expectations will be achieved, and actual results may be significantly different than that shown here. The duration and characteristics of past market/economic cycles and market behavior, including any bull/bear markets, is no indication of the duration and characteristics of any current or future be market/economic cycles or behavior.
